name: Teochew Steamed Pomfret
description: A clean, comforting steam that lets the silver pomfret's delicate sweetness lead, with salted vegetable, tomato, sour plum and tofu for a gentle savoury-sour broth.

ingredients.name: Whole silver pomfret, cleaned and scored
Teochew salted vegetable (kiam chai), rinsed and sliced

steps: Pat the fish dry and lay it on a heatproof plate deep enough to hold the juices.
Scatter the salted vegetable, ginger, mushrooms, tomato, tofu and sour plums around and over the fish. Lightly crush the plums.
Drizzle over the soy sauce and wine.
Steam over rapidly boiling water for 8–10 minutes, until the flesh at the thickest part turns opaque and pulls from the bone.
Spoon the tangy broth over the fish and serve immediately with rice.
tip: Don't oversteam — pomfret flesh dries out fast. Pull it the moment the centre turns opaque.
inspiredBy: A Teochew home-cooking classic; similar to versions by The Burning Kitchen and Spice N Pans.

name: Assam (Tamarind) Fish Curry
description: A sour-spicy tamarind curry built on a fragrant rempah, perfect for firm red fish, batang steaks or a meaty fish head. Tangy, rich and made for rice.

ingredients.name: Firm fish steaks or fish head
Tamarind pulp, soaked in 400 ml water and strained

steps: Blend the dried chillies, shallots, garlic, lemongrass and belacan into a smooth rempah.
Fry the rempah in oil over medium-low heat until the oil splits and it smells fragrant, 8–10 minutes.
Pour in the strained tamarind water and bring to a simmer. Season with sugar and salt to balance the sourness.
Add the okra and tomatoes, simmer 3 minutes, then slide in the fish.
Simmer gently until the fish is just cooked through, 6–8 minutes, basting rather than stirring so it doesn't break up. Rest 10 minutes before serving — the gravy deepens.
tip: Use a firm fish like batang, red snapper or grouper so the pieces hold together in the gravy.
inspiredBy: A Nonya assam pedas style curry, in the spirit of The Meatmen and local home recipes.

name: BBQ Sambal Stingray
description: Smoky grilled stingray wing smothered in a thick, fiery sambal and finished with calamansi and chinchalok. The quintessential East Coast hawker supper.

ingredients.name: Sambal (dried chilli, shallots, garlic, belacan, blended and fried)

steps: Rinse the stingray wing and pat dry. Lay it on a softened banana leaf.
Spread a thick, even layer of sambal over both sides of the wing.
Grill over charcoal or in a hot oven/griddle, leaf and all, about 6–8 minutes a side, until the flesh flakes into strands along the cartilage.
Serve straight off the leaf with a squeeze of calamansi, raw shallots and chinchalok on the side.
tip: The flesh is done when it peels cleanly off the cartilage in soft fibres — don't wait for browning.
inspiredBy: The classic hawker-stall preparation; close to versions shared by The Meatmen.

name: Sliced Fish Soup
description: A light, milky-sweet broth with tender slices of batang, tomato, lettuce and a hit of ginger — a hawker-centre comfort bowl you can make cleaner at home.

ingredients.name: Light soy, white pepper, sesame oil

steps: Toss the fish slices with a little cornflour, white pepper and sesame oil; set aside.
Bring the stock to a simmer with the ginger and tomato. Season with light soy.
Add the greens and let them wilt, then turn the heat to low.
Slip in the fish slices and poach gently for 1–2 minutes, just until opaque — do not boil hard or they'll toughen.
Stir in evaporated milk if using, and finish with white pepper. Serve over rice or thick bee hoon.
tip: Slice the fish across the grain and poach off a gentle simmer so the pieces stay silky.
inspiredBy: A home version of the hawker 'sliced fish soup', similar to Spice N Pans.

name: Cantonese Steamed Threadfin with Ginger & Scallion
description: The purest way to honour a premium fish: steamed threadfin dressed in hot oil, ginger, spring onion and a sweet-savoury soy. Restaurant results in 20 minutes.
steps: Lay the sliced ginger and white parts of the spring onion on a plate and rest the fish on top so steam circulates underneath.
Steam over high heat 7–9 minutes, until just opaque. Pour away the watery liquid that collects.
Top the fish with the julienned ginger and green spring onion.
Warm the soy, water and sugar together and pour around the fish.
Heat the oil until smoking and pour it over the aromatics so they sizzle. Garnish with coriander.
tip: Pour off the steaming liquid before saucing — it's the secret to a clean-tasting finish.
inspiredBy: Classic Cantonese steamed fish technique.

name: Singapore Chilli Crab
description: The national dish: meaty mud crab in a sweet, savoury, gently spicy tomato-chilli gravy thickened with egg, made for mopping up with fried mantou.

ingredients.name: Live mud crab, cleaned and chopped into sections
Fresh red chilli + dried chilli paste

steps: Lightly fry or steam the crab pieces until the shells just turn orange; set aside.
Fry the garlic, ginger and chilli paste in oil until fragrant and the oil reddens.
Stir in the ketchup, chilli sauce, sugar and soy, then the water. Bring to a simmer and taste — it should be sweet, savoury and tangy.
Return the crab, cover and simmer 5–6 minutes so the meat cooks through and soaks up the gravy.
Thicken with a little cornflour slurry, then turn off the heat and stream in the beaten egg, stirring to make silky ribbons. Serve with mantou.
tip: Crack the claws before saucing so the gravy gets inside, and add the egg off the heat for soft ribbons.
inspiredBy: The Singapore hawker icon; in the spirit of The Meatmen and Spice N Pans.

name: Black Pepper Crab
description: The drier, punchier cousin of chilli crab — crab sections wok-tossed in a buttery, intensely peppery sauce that clings to every shell.
steps: Toast the cracked pepper in a dry wok until aromatic, then set aside.
Lightly fry the crab pieces until the shells turn orange; remove.
Melt the butter, fry the garlic, ginger, curry leaves and chilli until fragrant.
Add the toasted pepper, oyster sauce, dark soy and sugar; stir into a glossy paste.
Return the crab and toss over high heat until well coated and cooked through, 4–5 minutes. Serve at once.
tip: Crack the pepper yourself and toast it — pre-ground pepper just can't deliver the same heat and perfume.
inspiredBy: A zi char and seafood-restaurant staple.

name: Butter Cereal Prawns
description: Crisp prawns tumbled in a buttery, curry-leaf-scented cereal crumble with a whisper of chilli — sweet, savoury and impossible to stop eating.
steps: Pat the prawns very dry and shallow-fry until crisp and bright orange; drain.
In a clean wok, melt the butter and fry the curry leaves and chilli until fragrant and the leaves crisp.
Add the cereal with a pinch of sugar, salt and milk powder; toast gently until golden and sandy.
Return the prawns and toss until every piece is coated in the crunchy cereal. Serve immediately while crisp.
tip: Keep the shells on — they crisp up and carry flavour, and they protect the sweet meat from drying out.
inspiredBy: A zi char favourite, similar to The Meatmen's cereal prawns.

name: Salted Egg Yolk Prawns
description: Golden, grainy salted-egg sauce foaming with butter and curry leaves, clinging to crisp shell-on prawns. Rich, creamy and addictive.

ingredients.name: Salted egg yolks, steamed and mashed

steps: Dust the prawns lightly with cornflour and fry until crisp; drain.
Melt the butter and fry the curry leaves and chilli until fragrant.
Add the mashed salted egg yolks and stir until they foam up and smell nutty.
Splash in the evaporated milk with a little sugar to loosen into a creamy sauce.
Toss the prawns through until fully coated. Serve straight away.
tip: Let the yolks foam in the butter before adding liquid — that foaming stage is where the flavour develops.
inspiredBy: The ubiquitous zi char salted egg treatment.

name: Sambal Sotong
description: Springy squid or cuttlefish in a glossy, sweet-spicy sambal slicked with tamarind and onions — a rice-disappearing dish in the best way.

ingredients.name: Squid or cuttlefish, cleaned and scored

steps: Blend the chillies, shallots, garlic and belacan into a rempah.
Fry the rempah in oil over medium heat until the oil splits and it darkens, 8–10 minutes.
Stir in the tamarind paste and sugar; taste for a sweet-sour-spicy balance.
Add the sliced onion and cook until soft, then turn the heat to high.
Toss in the squid and stir-fry just 2–3 minutes until it curls and turns opaque. Don't overcook or it goes rubbery.
tip: Score the squid in a crosshatch and cook it fast over high heat so it curls prettily and stays tender.
inspiredBy: A Malay-Peranakan home-style sambal dish.

name: Orh Luak (Oyster Omelette)
description: Crispy-edged, gooey-centred sweet potato starch omelette studded with plump oysters, finished with garlic chilli sauce. Pure hawker nostalgia.
steps: Whisk the sweet potato starch with the water into a thin batter.
Heat plenty of oil in a wok, fry the garlic, then pour in the starch batter and let it set and crisp at the edges.
Crack the eggs over the top, break the yolks and let them set into the crispy pancake.
Push everything aside, add the oysters and a splash of fish sauce, then fold together so the oysters stay just-cooked and juicy.
Plate with the crispy bits up, garnish, and serve with garlic-chilli sauce.
tip: Add the oysters near the very end — they only need a minute, and overcooking shrinks and toughens them.
inspiredBy: The hawker oyster omelette, Teochew/Hokkien style.

name: Stir-fried Cockles with Kang Kong
description: Just-blanched blood cockles tossed with water spinach in a punchy sambal-belacan — quick, briny and deeply satisfying.
steps: Blanch the cockles in just-boiled water for 10–20 seconds, then shell them, keeping any juice.
Get a wok smoking hot. Fry the garlic, sambal and taucheo until fragrant.
Add the kang kong stems first, then the leaves, and toss over high heat until just wilted.
Add the cockles and their juice with a pinch of sugar, toss for 20–30 seconds to warm through, and serve immediately.
tip: Cockles cook in seconds — they go in last, just to warm through, or they turn rubbery and dry.
inspiredBy: A hawker-style sambal kang kong with cockles.

name: Garlic & Wine Steamed Lala
description: Clams (or mussels and razor clams) steamed open in a fragrant broth of garlic, ginger, Chinese wine and glass noodles that soak up all the sweetness.

ingredients.name: Lala clams (or mussels / razor clams), purged

steps: Soak the clams in salted water for an hour to purge sand, then drain.
Lay the soaked glass noodles in a heatproof bowl. Top with garlic, ginger and the clams.
Pour over the stock, wine and soy.
Steam over high heat 6–8 minutes, until the shells open and the noodles are slippery and flavour-soaked.
Discard any unopened shells, scatter with chilli and spring onion, and serve in the bowl.
tip: Discard any shellfish that won't open after steaming — and any that were already open and won't close before cooking.
inspiredBy: A zi char steamed-clam preparation.

name: Sweet & Sour Crispy Tilapia
description: Whole tilapia fried until shatteringly crisp, then bathed in a glossy sweet-sour sauce with pineapple and peppers. A kid-friendly family favourite.
steps: Score the fish to the bone, pat dry and dust all over (and into the cuts) with cornflour.
Deep-fry in hot oil until golden and crisp, 6–8 minutes, then drain on a rack.
In a wok, fry the onion and capsicum briefly, then add ketchup, vinegar, sugar and a splash of water.
Add the pineapple, bring to a simmer and thicken with the slurry into a glossy sauce.
Pour the hot sauce over the crispy fish just before serving so it stays crunchy.
tip: Sauce the fish at the table or right before eating — pour too early and the lovely crust goes soggy.
inspiredBy: A classic Cantonese-style sweet and sour whole fish.

name: Steamed Grouper in Superior Soy
description: Banquet-style steamed grouper (or snapper, seabass) with hot oil, ginger, spring onion and a sweet superior soy — the gold standard for a sweet, gelatinous fish.

ingredients.name: Superior light soy (or light soy + sugar)

steps: Rest the fish on sliced ginger and spring onion whites on a plate so steam flows underneath.
Steam over high heat 8–10 minutes (longer for a whole fish), until the flesh just flakes at the bone.
Tip away the steaming liquid. Crown the fish with julienned ginger and green spring onion.
Warm the soy with the water and a touch of sugar; pour around the fish.
Pour smoking-hot oil over the aromatics to release their fragrance. Garnish and serve.
tip: A whole grouper's head and skin are gelatinous and prized — steam just to set, never past flaky.
inspiredBy: The classic Cantonese steamed fish, restaurant style.

name: Crispy Fried Fish with Sambal
description: Small whole fish — ikan kuning, kembong or milkfish — marinated in turmeric and fried shatteringly crisp, served with a sharp sambal. Bones and all.

ingredients.name: Small whole fish, scaled and gutted
Sambal belacan or sambal chilli, to serve

steps: Pat the fish very dry, then rub inside and out with turmeric and salt. Rest 15 minutes.
Heat the oil until shimmering hot — a small piece of fish should sizzle immediately.
Fry the fish in batches without crowding, 4–6 minutes a side, until deeply golden and crisp.
Drain on a rack (not paper, so they stay crisp). Serve with sambal, calamansi and cucumber.
tip: Dry fish and hot oil are everything — moisture makes the oil spit and the skin go soft instead of crisp.
inspiredBy: The nasi-lemak-style fried fish found at Malay stalls everywhere.

name: Braised Abalone & Sea Cucumber
description: A Chinese New Year treasure dish: tender abalone and silky sea cucumber braised with mushrooms in a glossy superior-stock gravy. Prized for texture and prosperity.
steps: Cut the softened sea cucumber into thick pieces. Blanch with ginger and wine to remove any odour, then drain.
Fry the ginger and spring onion in oil, add the mushrooms, then pour in the stock, oyster sauce, dark soy and wine.
Add the sea cucumber and simmer gently, covered, for 45–60 minutes until silky and the gravy reduces.
Add the abalone in the last 10 minutes (fresh) or just to warm through (canned) — long cooking toughens it.
Thicken the gravy with a little slurry until glossy, and serve, often over blanched greens.
tip: Buy sea cucumber pre-soaked from the dried-goods stall — rehydrating it from scratch takes 2–3 days of daily water changes.
inspiredBy: A classic Cantonese banquet / Chinese New Year braise.

name: Boiled Gong Gong with Chilli Dip
description: The ultimate beer-time nibble: dog conch simply boiled with aromatics, the sweet meat twisted out with a toothpick and dunked in a tangy chilli sauce.

ingredients.name: Red chilli, garlic, calamansi, sugar (blended dip)

steps: Soak the gong gong in salted water for 30 minutes so they purge sand, then scrub the shells.
Bring a pot of water with ginger, lemongrass and salt to a rolling boil.
Tip in the gong gong and boil 4–6 minutes, until the meat just sets — don't overboil or it turns rubbery.
Meanwhile blend the chilli, garlic, sugar and calamansi into a tangy dipping sauce.
Serve hot in a bowl with toothpicks — twist the meat out of each shell and dip.
tip: Twist, don't yank — turning the toothpick as you pull keeps the curl of meat whole instead of snapping off the tip.
inspiredBy: The classic zi char / seafood-stall gong gong.

name: Singapore Fish Head Curry
description: A big red snapper head simmered in a tangy, spiced coconut-and-tamarind curry with okra and brinjal — a multicultural Singapore icon, rich and made for rice.

ingredients.name: Red snapper or grouper head, halved
Tamarind pulp in 300 ml water, strained

steps: Mix the curry powder with a little water into a paste. Splutter the mustard seeds and curry leaves in oil, then fry the blended aromatics until fragrant.
Add the curry paste and cook until the oil separates, 5–8 minutes.
Pour in the tamarind water, bring to a simmer, then add the vegetables.
Slide in the fish head, spoon gravy over it, cover and simmer 12–15 minutes until cooked.
Stir in the coconut milk, warm through without boiling hard, balance with salt and sugar, and rest 10 minutes before serving.
tip: Add the coconut milk at the end and don't boil it hard, or it splits and turns grainy.
inspiredBy: Singapore's beloved fish head curry, where South Indian and Chinese cooking meet.

name: Otah-Otah (Grilled Spiced Fishcake)
description: Smooth spiced fish paste bound with coconut and egg, wrapped in banana leaf and grilled until just set — smoky, fragrant and a little spicy.

ingredients.name: Rempah (chilli, shallot, lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, candlenut)

steps: Fry the rempah in a little oil until fragrant and cooled.
Mix the minced fish with the rempah, coconut milk, egg, sugar and salt into a smooth, slightly sticky paste.
Fold in the shredded leaves.
Spoon onto banana leaf rectangles, fold into parcels and secure.
Grill or pan-roast the parcels over medium heat, turning, until the leaf chars and the filling is just set, about 8–10 minutes.
tip: Don't overfill the parcels — a thin layer cooks evenly and stays moist; thick ones turn dense.
inspiredBy: Nonya otah, a staple of Peranakan and hawker cooking.

name: Homemade Bouncy Fishballs
description: Springy, snow-white fishballs made the old way — scraped fish flesh beaten with iced water and salt until bouncy, then poached. The heart of fishball noodles.

ingredients.name: Fish flesh (wolf herring/yellowtail), scraped clean of bone

steps: Scrape the fish flesh off the skin and bones; you want clean, pale meat.
Add the salt and beat with iced water a little at a time, stirring vigorously in one direction until tacky and pale.
Mix in the starch, sugar and pepper, then throw the paste against the bowl repeatedly until very springy.
Wet your hand, squeeze paste through your fist and scoop balls with a spoon into a pot of barely simmering water.
Poach until they float and feel firm, then lift out. Use in soup or noodles.
tip: Keep everything cold and beat in one direction — that's what develops the signature bounce.
inspiredBy: Traditional Teochew fishball-making.

name: Hae Mee (Prawn Noodle Soup)
description: A deep, sweet-savoury prawn-and-pork broth built from prawn heads and shells, ladled over noodles with poached prawns and a spoon of fried shallots and chilli.

ingredients.name: Large prawns, heads and shells reserved

steps: Fry the prawn heads and shells hard in oil, crushing them, until deep orange and fragrant — this is the soul of the broth.
Add water and the pork ribs and simmer 1 hour; strain out the shells, pressing to extract every drop.
Season the stock with rock sugar, dark soy, salt and the toasted sole fish.
Poach the peeled prawns in the stock just until they curl; remove.
Blanch noodles and vegetables, top with prawns and broth, and finish with fried shallots and sambal.
tip: Really fry the prawn shells until they smell toasty before adding water — that roasting is where the colour and depth come from.
inspiredBy: Singapore hawker prawn mee.

name: Katong Laksa
description: Rich, coconutty laksa gravy fragrant with dried shrimp and laksa leaf, over thick rice noodles with prawns, cockles, fishcake and a swirl of sambal.

ingredients.name: Laksa rempah (dried chilli, shallot, lemongrass, galangal, candlenut, belacan, dried shrimp)

steps: Make a prawn-shell stock; peel and poach the prawns and set aside.
Fry the rempah in oil until the oil splits and it smells deep and fragrant, 10 minutes.
Add the stock and simmer, then stir in the coconut milk and laksa leaf; don't boil hard.
Season with salt and a little sugar to balance.
Pile noodles, prawns, cockles, fishcake, tau pok and beansprouts in a bowl, ladle over the gravy, and top with sambal.
tip: Blanch the cockles for just 15 seconds and add at the end — laksa gravy is hot enough to finish them.
inspiredBy: Singapore's Peranakan laksa lemak.

name: Char Kway Teow
description: Smoky wok-fried flat rice noodles with prawns, cockles, egg, beansprouts and chives in a dark sweet-savoury sauce — all about the 'wok hei'.

ingredients.name: Dark soy, light soy, sweet sauce, sambal

steps: Get the wok blazing hot. Fry garlic and lap cheong, then the prawns briefly.
Add the noodles and the dark/light/sweet sauces; toss hard so they char slightly.
Push aside, scramble the egg, then fold everything together.
Add beansprouts, chives, cockles and a spoon of sambal; toss for 20–30 seconds and serve at once.
Cook in single portions — a crowded wok steams instead of chars.
tip: Add the cockles last, off the heat almost — residual heat warms them without making them rubbery.
inspiredBy: The hawker classic, prized for its smoky wok hei.

name: White Pepper Prawns
description: Shell-on prawns wok-tossed in a peppery, buttery, savoury sauce with loads of freshly ground white pepper — simple, punchy and addictive.
steps: Pat the prawns dry and sear in a little oil until they just turn orange; remove.
Melt the butter, fry the garlic, then add a generous amount of white pepper and let it toast briefly.
Add oyster sauce, soy and sugar to form a glossy sauce.
Return the prawns and toss until coated and cooked through.
Finish with spring onion and even more pepper to taste.
tip: Grind the white pepper fresh and use more than feels sensible — it's the whole point of the dish.
inspiredBy: A zi char white pepper crab/prawn treatment.

name: Teochew Cold Cuttlefish
description: Cuttlefish poached to tender perfection, chilled and sliced, served cold with a tangy garlic-chilli or sweet-sour dip — a clean, refined Teochew classic.

ingredients.name: Garlic, red chilli, sugar, vinegar (for the dip)

steps: Bring water with ginger, spring onion and wine to a gentle simmer.
Poach the whole cleaned cuttlefish gently — do not boil hard — until just opaque and tender, 3–5 minutes depending on size.
Plunge immediately into iced water to stop cooking and keep it tender and crisp.
Slice thinly across the body.
Blend the dip and serve the cold cuttlefish with it.
tip: Gentle poach then ice bath — a hard boil is what turns cuttlefish into rubber bands.
inspiredBy: A Teochew braised/cold seafood platter staple.

name: Sotong Kang Kong (with Hae Ko)
description: Blanched squid and water spinach dressed in a sweet, dark prawn-paste (hae ko) sauce with sesame and chilli — a beloved cold-ish hawker plate.
steps: Blanch the kang kong briefly until just tender-crisp, then drain and cool.
Blanch the scored squid for under a minute, until it just curls; drain.
Loosen the hae ko with a little hot water, sugar and lime into a pourable sauce.
Arrange squid and kang kong, pour over the sauce, and top with sesame, sambal and you tiao pieces.
Toss gently just before eating.
tip: Score the squid and blanch it for seconds, not minutes — it should be silky, not chewy.
inspiredBy: The hawker sotong kang kong with prawn paste.

name: Ikan Bakar (Grilled Sambal Fish)
description: Whole fish or fillets lacquered with sambal, wrapped in banana leaf and grilled over charcoal until smoky and caramelised — the smell of the night market.

ingredients.name: Sambal (chilli, shallot, garlic, belacan, tamarind, sugar)

steps: Rub the fish with turmeric and salt; rest 15 minutes.
Slather generously with sambal on both sides (and inside, if whole).
Wrap in softened banana leaf, or lay on the leaf on the grill.
Grill over medium charcoal, turning once, until the leaf chars and the fish flakes, 6–10 minutes a side.
Serve with calamansi, raw shallots and cucumber.
tip: Oil the banana leaf and the grill so the skin doesn't stick and tear when you turn it.
inspiredBy: Malay-style grilled fish from pasar malam stalls.

name: Assam Pedas (Sour & Spicy Fish)
description: A fiery, intensely sour tamarind gravy — no coconut — perfumed with torch ginger and laksa leaf, perfect for oily fish like mackerel and sardine.

ingredients.name: Tamarind pulp in 400 ml water, strained

steps: Fry the rempah in oil until the oil splits and darkens.
Add the tamarind water and bring to a simmer; season to a bold sour-spicy balance.
Add the torch ginger, laksa leaf, okra and tomato; simmer a few minutes.
Slide in the fish and simmer gently until just cooked, basting rather than stirring.
Rest briefly and serve — the gravy should be punchy, sour and red.
tip: Torch ginger and laksa leaf are what make assam pedas taste like assam pedas — don't skip them.
inspiredBy: Malay assam pedas, a sour-spicy cousin of fish curry.

name: Grilled Glazed Eel (Unagi Kabayaki)
description: Butterflied eel grilled and repeatedly brushed with a sweet-savoury soy glaze until lacquered and caramelised, served over rice as unadon.

ingredients.name: Eel, cleaned and butterflied (ask the fishmonger)

steps: Simmer the soy, mirin, sake and sugar into a glossy tare (glaze) that coats a spoon.
Steam or par-grill the eel skin-side first to render some fat and firm it up.
Grill flesh-side, brushing on the tare and turning, building up several caramelised layers.
Slice and lay over hot rice, brush with more glaze.
Finish with a pinch of sansho pepper.
tip: Build the glaze in thin layers, letting each set on the grill — one thick coat just slides off and burns.
inspiredBy: Japanese unagi no kabayaki, popular across Singapore.

name: Chilled Jellyfish Salad
description: Crunchy shredded jellyfish dressed in sesame oil, vinegar and a little chilli — a cooling, textural appetiser found on every zi char and banquet table.
steps: Rinse the salted jellyfish thoroughly and soak in cold water 1–2 hours, changing the water, to remove salt.
Blanch very briefly in warm (not boiling) water — it will curl — then plunge into iced water; it stays crunchy.
Drain very well and squeeze out excess water.
Toss with sesame oil, vinegar, soy, sugar, garlic and chilli.
Chill, then garnish with sesame and coriander before serving.
tip: Use warm water, never boiling — boiling shrinks jellyfish to nothing and kills the crunch.
inspiredBy: The classic Chinese cold jellyfish appetiser.

name: Salmon Fish Head Bee Hoon Soup
description: A milky, comforting bowl of bee hoon in a sweet ginger broth with crispy fried salmon head pieces, tomato and greens — a modern hawker favourite.

ingredients.name: Salmon head and collar, in pieces
Salt, white pepper, Hua Diao wine

steps: Pat the salmon pieces dry and pan-fry or deep-fry until golden and crisp; set aside.
Simmer the stock with ginger and a splash of wine; add the tomato.
Add the bee hoon and greens and cook until tender.
Stir in the evaporated milk and season; warm without boiling hard.
Add the fried salmon pieces just before serving so they keep some crispness.
tip: Fry the salmon head first and add it at the end — it flavours the bowl while staying crisp at the edges.
inspiredBy: The popular salmon fish head bee hoon found in coffeeshops.

name: Pan-Seared Teriyaki Salmon
description: Crisp-skinned salmon (or cobia/amberjack) glazed in a quick homemade teriyaki — weeknight-fast, with a glossy sweet-savoury finish.
steps: Pat the fillets very dry and season lightly with salt.
Sear skin-side down in oil over medium heat, pressing gently, until the skin is crisp; flip and cook to just done.
Mix soy, mirin, sake and sugar; pour into the pan.
Let it bubble and reduce, spooning the glaze over the fish until glossy.
Garnish with sesame and spring onion; serve with rice.
tip: Dry skin + medium heat + patience = crackly skin; moving the fish too early tears it.
inspiredBy: Home-style Japanese teriyaki.

name: Tom Yum Talay (Seafood)
description: A hot-and-sour Thai broth loud with lemongrass, galangal, lime and chilli, full of prawns, squid and mussels — clear or creamy, your call.

ingredients.name: Nam prik pao (chilli jam) and fish sauce

steps: Simmer stock or water with lemongrass, galangal and lime leaves to infuse, 10 minutes.
Stir in the nam prik pao, mushrooms and tomato.
Add the seafood in order of cooking time — fish and prawns first, squid and mussels last.
As soon as everything is just cooked, turn off the heat.
Season off the heat with fish sauce, lime juice and a little sugar; taste for hot-sour-salty balance.
tip: Add the lime juice at the very end off the heat — boiling it makes the soup bitter instead of bright.
inspiredBy: Thai tom yum, a Southeast Asian seafood staple.

name: Thai Steamed Fish with Lime & Garlic
description: A whole seabass steamed and drenched in a punchy, sour-spicy garlic-lime-chilli sauce (pla neung manao) — bright, fiery and incredibly fresh-tasting.

ingredients.name: Whole seabass or snapper, cleaned and scored

steps: Lay the fish on a plate over a bed of lemongrass and steam over high heat until just opaque, 10–12 minutes.
Pound or blend the garlic and chilli; mix with lime juice, fish sauce and sugar into a sharp dressing.
Pour off the steaming liquid.
Drench the hot fish in the lime-garlic sauce.
Shower with coriander and serve immediately.
tip: Make the sauce fierce and tangy — it mellows the second it hits the hot fish.
inspiredBy: Thai pla neung manao.

name: Drunken Prawns
description: Live prawns flash-cooked in an aromatic broth heady with Chinese wine and wolfberries — sweet, clean and warming, the wine doing the heavy lifting.
steps: Simmer the stock with ginger, goji berries and red dates for 10 minutes to build an aromatic base.
Season lightly with salt and a little sugar.
Add the prawns and most of the wine; cover and cook just until the prawns curl and turn orange.
Splash in the remaining wine at the end for fragrance.
Serve in the broth, hot.
tip: Add a final hit of wine right at the end — cooking it all off loses the fragrant 'drunken' aroma.
inspiredBy: The classic Chinese drunken prawns.

name: Salt & Pepper Squid
description: Crisp, light-battered squid tossed with fried garlic, chilli and a salty-peppery seasoning — the ultimate beer snack, shatteringly crunchy.

ingredients.name: Squid, cleaned and cut into rings

steps: Pat the squid very dry and toss in the combined flours with half the salt and pepper.
Deep-fry in hot oil in batches for about 1–2 minutes until pale gold and crisp; drain.
In a little oil, fry the garlic, chilli and spring onion until fragrant.
Return the squid, sprinkle the remaining salt and pepper, and toss quickly.
Serve immediately while crunchy, with lime.
tip: Fry hot and fast — squid only needs a minute or two; longer and the tender rings turn to rubber under the crust.
inspiredBy: Cantonese salt-and-pepper seafood.

name: Stir-fried Scallops in XO Sauce
description: Plump scallops seared and tossed with spicy, umami-rich XO sauce, asparagus and garlic — a quick, restaurant-style stir-fry.
steps: Sear the dried scallops hard in a hot pan for under a minute a side, just to colour; remove.
Stir-fry the garlic, ginger and vegetables until bright and crisp-tender.
Add the XO sauce and wine and let it sizzle.
Return the scallops, season with soy and sugar, and thicken lightly with slurry.
Toss to coat and serve at once — the scallops should stay just-set and juicy.
tip: Dry the scallops well and sear briefly — overcooked scallops shrink and turn tough and watery.
inspiredBy: Cantonese XO sauce seafood stir-fry.

name: Mussels in Coconut Curry
description: Mussels steamed open in a fragrant lemongrass-coconut curry broth with chilli and lime — quick, generous and made for dunking bread or rice.
steps: Fry the garlic, shallot and curry paste in oil until fragrant.
Add the lemongrass, lime leaf and coconut milk; bring to a simmer and season with fish sauce and sugar.
Tip in the mussels, cover, and steam 4–5 minutes until they open.
Discard any that stay shut; squeeze in lime.
Scatter with Thai basil and chilli and serve with the broth.
tip: Discard mussels that don't open after cooking — and any that won't close when tapped raw beforehand.
inspiredBy: A Southeast Asian coconut-curry mussel pot.

name: Silky Steamed Egg with Seafood
description: Custard-smooth savoury steamed egg studded with clams and prawns — gentle, comforting and a brilliant way to show off very fresh shellfish.

ingredients.name: Warm stock or water (1.5x the egg volume)

steps: Beat the eggs with the warm stock and a pinch of salt; skim off bubbles and strain for silkiness.
Pour into a shallow dish, cover with foil or a plate.
Steam over low-medium heat 8 minutes until just set but still wobbly.
Tuck in the clams and prawns and steam another 4–5 minutes until they open and the egg is set.
Finish with soy, sesame oil and spring onion.
tip: Gentle heat and a covered dish keep the surface mirror-smooth — high heat makes it pockmarked and watery.
inspiredBy: Chinese steamed egg (zheng shui dan).

name: Sambal Petai Prawns
description: Prawns and pungent 'stink beans' (petai) tossed in a sweet, spicy tamarind sambal — bold, funky and a serious rice-killer.
steps: Fry the sambal in oil over medium heat until the oil splits and it smells deep, 8–10 minutes.
Stir in the tamarind paste and sugar; taste for a sweet-sour-spicy balance.
Add the onion and petai and stir-fry until the beans are bright and just tender.
Turn up the heat, add the prawns and toss until they curl and turn orange.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
tip: Add the prawns last and cook briefly — they only need a couple of minutes once the sambal is ready.
inspiredBy: The Malay sambal udang petai.

name: Sea Urchin Rice Bowl (Uni Don)
description: Fresh sea urchin roe laid over warm seasoned rice with nori and a touch of soy and wasabi — minimal effort, maximum luxury, all about the uni.
steps: Season warm rice lightly with a splash of sushi vinegar if you like, and let it cool to just-warm.
Scatter shredded nori over the rice.
Gently lay the uni lobes on top, taking care not to break them.
Add a tiny dab of wasabi and a few drops of soy — go light, the uni is the star.
Garnish and eat immediately while the uni is cold and the rice is warm.
tip: Use the freshest, firmest uni you can get and dress it barely — soy should whisper, not shout.
inspiredBy: Japanese uni donburi.

name: Geoduck Sashimi & Hotpot
description: A two-ways treatment for a single prized clam: the crunchy siphon sliced raw for sashimi, the rest swished in a clear hotpot — a banquet showpiece.
steps: Blanch the geoduck siphon for 10 seconds, then plunge into iced water and peel off the dark outer skin.
Slice the cleaned, crunchy siphon very thinly across for sashimi; arrange on ice.
Cut the body meat into bite-size pieces for the hotpot.
Simmer the clear stock with ginger, spring onion and goji berries.
Swish the body (and scallops) in the hot stock for seconds until just opaque; eat the sashimi with the dip.
tip: The siphon's whole appeal is its crunch — for sashimi it's barely blanched and iced, never cooked through.
inspiredBy: The classic Chinese restaurant way with elephant trunk clam.
